what_it_does: Defines and maintains the shared vocabulary, operation verbs, named-act registry, error-envelope contract, cursor/pagination rules, address grammar, purpose-free vocabulary, and journey ambiguity classes for the company UI contract; it is a living, append-only conventions document that records both target shapes and current implementation gaps.
observations: Closed uniform op verb set: list, get, create, update, delete, act, watch, search.
Append-only named-act registry with per-lane additions (F3 execution/control, F7 reach, F4 extension fabric, F1/F2 resource control).
Explicit field-reality honesty: the target error envelope is not yet implemented and each face emits a different carrier.
Cursor/pagination law uses seq-cursor, oldest-first, client-held cursors and durable per-consumer refs.
Address grammar defines live schemes session:// and cas://, with msg://, thread://, consumer:// planned.
Purpose-free vocabulary bans common UX words and allows lint-ok carve-outs for native Claude Code identifiers.
Journey ambiguity classes each map to a specific journey file for disambiguation.
Entries carry implementation status labels such as planned, building, and re-exposed.
Auth acts were reopened as buildable for a sole-operator model with consent-not-lockdown.
The document follows a no-versioning law and is updated in place.
standouts: The append-only named-act registry spanning multiple lanes with explicit implementation status.
The field-reality honesty section admitting the target error envelope is not implemented anywhere.
The purpose-free vocabulary law with lint-ok carve-outs for native identifiers.
The sole-operator override that enables auth acts previously treated as out of scope.
conventions: Follows a closed, uniform verb vocabulary across all faces.
Uses append-only registries and in-place updates with no versioning.
Cites external contract-format sections for obligations.
Names implementation gaps explicitly rather than hiding them.
Breaks typical UI vocabulary by banning words like button, panel, screen, click, frontend, page.
concerns: The append-only registry may accumulate obsolete or superseded acts without a retirement mechanism.
A large portion of the named acts are marked planned, indicating the contract runs ahead of implementation.
The target error envelope is widely unmet, creating a contract-versus-reality gap.
Inline lint-ok carve-outs may weaken vocabulary enforcement if overused.
The recent auth override (2026-06-13) could drift against earlier architecture rulings.
notes: A living, append-only contract dictionary that keeps the UI contract honest about what is implemented versus what is only planned.
questions: What mechanism enforces the purpose-free vocabulary ban across prose?
How are planned acts promoted to building or implemented, and where is that lifecycle tracked?
How does this file relate to the separate contract-format sections it cites?
How would the sole-operator auth model change if multi-user scenarios are introduced later?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is to provide uniform vocabulary, envelopes, and laws; the file does exactly that, while also explicitly documenting where current implementation diverges from the target contract.
intention_signals: It tries to stabilize a large, multi-lane API surface by enforcing a small shared language, recording every named operation in a single append-only registry, and making implementation gaps visible rather than pretending unimplemented contract obligations already exist.
novelty: The deliberate field-reality honesty section that catalogs the exact mismatch between the target error envelope and current face carriers, plus the purpose-free vocabulary law with lint-ok carve-outs for native identifiers.
contribution: A centralized, living vocabulary and named-act registry that gives every lane a consistent way to name operations, plus an explicit record of which contract obligations are implemented and which remain gaps, so a fused system can reason about the surface without re-inventing verbs or mistaking plans for shipped behavior.
summary_for_embedding: A living conventions document that defines the uniform verbs, named-act registry, error-envelope target, cursor/pagination law, address grammar, purpose-free vocabulary, and journey ambiguity classes for the company UI contract, while honestly marking implementation status.
intention_for_embedding: To enforce semantic consistency across the UI contract surface and keep the contract honest about what is implemented versus planned.
